nostalgia.funFact: This iconic sound was actually a handshake between two modems negotiating the fastest possible connection speed!

nostalgia.question: What popular stretchy, plastic jewelry often adorned the necks and wrists of 90s kids, sometimes appearing to 'disappear' into the skin?
nostalgia.funFact: These chokers were inspired by traditional Polynesian tattoo designs and made a huge comeback in the 2010s!

nostalgia.question: What beloved green ogre, voiced by Mike Myers, first graced the big screen in 2001, proving that beauty is on the inside?
nostalgia.funFact: The original Shrek film won the very first Academy Award for Best Animated Feature!

nostalgia.question: Which boy band famously gave us hits like 'I Want It That Way' and 'Everybody (Backstreet's Back)'?
nostalgia.funFact: The Backstreet Boys were named after a famous flea market in Orlando, Florida, called the 'Backstreet Market.'

nostalgia.question: Before becoming a huge movie star, which actor got his start as the charming Fresh Prince of Bel-Air?
nostalgia.funFact: The show's premise was actually based on producer Benny Medina's own life story of moving from a rough neighborhood to Beverly Hills.

nostalgia.question: What iconic blue hedgehog was the main rival to Nintendo's Mario during the 90s console wars?
nostalgia.funFact: Sonic was originally conceived as a rabbit before evolving into the super-fast hedgehog we know today!

nostalgia.question: In the 1995 animated film 'Toy Story,' what was the name of the cowboy doll who felt threatened by the arrival of Buzz Lightyear?
nostalgia.funFact: Tom Hanks, who voiced Woody, recorded his lines in a special isolated booth, separate from the other actors, to give his performance a unique energy.

nostalgia.question: What popular instant messaging service, launched by AOL in 1997, used 'buddy lists' and distinctive sounds?
nostalgia.funFact: The iconic 'You've Got Mail!' soundbite was so famous that it even became the title of a popular movie starring Tom Hanks and Meg Ryan!

nostalgia.question: Which cartoon followed the adventures of four elementary school kids on their school playground, often dealing with the unwritten rules of childhood?
nostalgia.funFact: Each of the six main characters in 'Recess' represented a different archetype of children found on a playground.

nostalgia.question: What pop-punk band, known for their green-haired frontman, released the massively successful album 'Dookie' in 1994?
nostalgia.funFact: The band originally formed under the name 'Sweet Children' before changing it to Green Day, a slang term for smoking marijuana all day.

nostalgia.question: What virtual pet, created by Bandai, required constant attention and feeding to prevent it from 'dying'?
nostalgia.funFact: The name 'Tamagotchi' is a portmanteau of the Japanese word 'tamago' (egg) and the English word 'watch.'

nostalgia.question: In 'Mean Girls' (2004), what day of the week did The Plastics famously wear pink?
nostalgia.funFact: 'Mean Girls' was written by Tina Fey, who also played Ms. Norbury in the film, and is loosely based on Rosalind Wiseman's book 'Queen Bees and Wannabes.'

nostalgia.question: What popular reality TV show, which premiered in 2002, followed a dysfunctional rockstar family led by Ozzy Osbourne?
nostalgia.funFact: 'The Osbournes' was one of MTV's most successful shows ever and won an Emmy Award for Outstanding Reality Program in 2002!

nostalgia.question: Which alternative rock band, known for the song 'Semi-Charmed Life,' had a unique sound featuring a 'kindergarten-teacher-turned-rapper'?
nostalgia.funFact: The band's frontman, Stephan Jenkins, originally conceived 'Semi-Charmed Life' as a rap song, which explains its fast-paced, rhythmic delivery.

nostalgia.question: In the early seasons of 'Friends,' what was the name of the coffee shop where the gang frequently hung out?
nostalgia.funFact: The orange couch at Central Perk was found in the basement of the Warner Bros. studio!

nostalgia.question: What iconic point-and-click adventure game from LucasArts tasked players with saving the world from purple tentacles in 1993?
nostalgia.funFact: 'Day of the Tentacle' was a sequel to the 1987 game 'Maniac Mansion' and allowed players to switch between three characters in different time periods!

nostalgia.question: What was the name of the first widely popular web browser, released in 1993, that paved the way for the graphical internet?
nostalgia.funFact: Mosaic was developed at the National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA) and was instrumental in popularizing the World Wide Web.

nostalgia.question: Which animated series featured a trio of misfits who schemed to get money for jawbreakers, often leading to painful outcomes?
nostalgia.funFact: Creator Danny Antonucci drew inspiration from his own childhood experiences and neighborhood friendships for the show.

nostalgia.question: What small, plastic, brightly colored alien figures, often with glitter or translucent bodies, were collected and traded by kids in the late 90s?
nostalgia.funFact: These figures were inspired by ancient game pieces from Spain called 'huesitos' (little bones).

nostalgia.question: In the 1996 film 'Space Jam,' what famous basketball player teamed up with the Looney Tunes to defeat the Monstars?
nostalgia.funFact: A special basketball court and gym, dubbed 'The Jordan Dome,' was built on the Warner Bros. lot so Michael Jordan could train during filming!

nostalgia.question: What Danish-Norwegian Europop group dominated the charts in the late 90s with their hit song 'Barbie Girl'?
nostalgia.funFact: The song 'Barbie Girl' led to a highly publicized lawsuit from Mattel, the creators of Barbie, which was eventually dismissed.

nostalgia.question: Before it was adapted into a major movie franchise, what popular trading card game involved duelists summoning monsters and using spell cards?
nostalgia.funFact: The creator, Kazuki Takahashi, originally intended 'Yu-Gi-Oh!' to be a horror manga, not a card game series, with the protagonist solving various games and puzzles.

nostalgia.question: What was the name of the digital camera accessory that allowed users to print tiny, sticky photos directly from their phone in the early 2000s, often associated with a certain flip phone?
nostalgia.funFact: The i-Zone printer was particularly popular for its compatibility with iconic flip phones like the Motorola Razr, letting users instantly share their lo-fi pics.
